summary: Unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond (also called olefins). Simplest example is ethylene. General formula CnH2n. Contain sp2-hybridized carbons.

works.name: Named Reactions Forming Alkenes
works.description: Reactions that produce alkenes as products.

clues.clue: McMurry reaction: titanium(III) chloride reductively couples carbonyls to form alkenes (variant of pinacol coupling)
examples: titanium(III) chloride reductively couples carbonyls to form these structures in the McMurry reaction
created in a modification of the pinacol coupling named for McMurry

clues.clue: Wittig reaction: phosphonium ylides react with ketones/aldehydes; favors Z-stereoisomer
examples: reaction of phosphonium ylides with ketones or aldehydes favors the production of the Z-stereoisomer

clues.clue: Peterson olefination: alpha-silyl carbanion added to carbonyl, followed by elimination
examples: begins with the addition of an alpha silyl carbanion to a carbonyl followed by an elimination step

clues.clue: Julia reaction: phenyl sulfones react with carbonyl compounds, reduced with sodium amalgam
examples: The Julia reaction reacts phenyl sulfones with carbonyl compounds and reduces the product with sodium amalgam

clues.clue: Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons (HWE) and Still-Gennari modification: stabilized phosphonate anion + carbonyl; Still-Gennari favors Z-alkenes using KHMDS and 18-crown-6
examples: stabilized phosphonate anion reacts with a carbonyl in the presence of KHMDS and 18-crown-6

clues.clue: Zaitsev's rule: elimination reactions favor the more-substituted alkene (opposite of Hofmann's rule)
examples: more substituted product is formed according to Zaitsev's rule
thermodynamic stability of these compounds increases with substitution

clues.clue: Shapiro, Bamford-Stevens, and Chugaev/Grieco eliminations also produce alkenes
examples: treatment of tosylhydrazones with strong bases produces these compounds
Chugaev and Grieco eliminations

works.name: Olefin Metathesis
works.description: Exchange of substituents between alkenes. A Nobel Prize-winning reaction.

clues.clue: Grubbs catalyst (ruthenium-based): preferred for metathesis due to air/water stability; second generation contains NHC ligand
examples: Grubbs' catalyst make two compounds with these structures exchange parts in a namesake metathesis
ruthenium-based catalyst is often preferred for alkene metathesis
Grubb's catalyst can be used to form rotaxanes via a clipping mechanism

clues.clue: Schrock catalyst (molybdenum-based): more active than Grubbs but less stable
examples: Schrock and Grubbs catalysts make two compounds exchange parts
Schrock's catalyst is more active

clues.clue: Metathesis involves formation of a metallacyclobutane intermediate
examples: combine with two alkenes to form a metallacyclobutane, which then breaks up

clues.clue: Carbenes (neutral carbon with 6 valence electrons) coordinated to Ru in Grubbs catalyst
examples: This functional group is coordinated to ruthenium in the Grubbs catalyst

works.name: Addition Reactions to Alkenes
works.description: Reactions that add groups across the double bond.

clues.clue: Markovnikov's rule: addition of HX/water gives the more-substituted product
examples: addition of halogens to this functional group is governed by Markovnikov's rule
Markovnikov addition of water gives an alcohol

clues.clue: Anti-Markovnikov: hydroboration-oxidation (BH3 in THF) gives anti-Markovnikov alcohol
examples: Treating this functional group with borane in THF yields the anti-Markovnikov product
hydroboration-oxidation converts alkenes to anti-Markovnikov alcohols

clues.clue: Oxymercuration-demercuration: mercury acetate + water, then NaBH4; Markovnikov alcohol without rearrangements
examples: this nucleophilic functional group displaces an acetoxy group after attacking mercury
a certain acetate reacts with an alkene to form a three membered ring

clues.clue: Bromine test: alkenes decolorize a solution of liquid bromine (canonical test for unsaturation)
examples: A solution of bromine is often used to detect this functional group
they rapidly decolorize liquid bromine
Halogens add to them in the canonical test for unsaturation

clues.clue: Epoxidation: peroxyacids convert alkenes to epoxides; Shi epoxidation uses ketone catalyst + Oxone
examples: peroxyacids convert to epoxides
Potassium peroxymonosulfate and a ketone catalyst react with these compounds

clues.clue: Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation: OsO4 + chiral quinine ligand → diols
examples: this functional group reacts with osmium tetroxide to form diols in an asymmetric reaction named for Sharpless
Osmium tetroxide catalyzes the dihydroxylation

clues.clue: Prevost and Woodward reactions: iodine + silver benzoate/acetate for diols
examples: treated with iodine and either silver benzoate or silver acetate in the Prevost and Woodward reactions

clues.clue: Carbenes add to alkenes to make cyclopropanes
examples: Carbenes add to these compounds to make cyclopropanes

clues.clue: Radical initiators (peroxides) reverse regioselectivity: anti-Markovnikov HBr addition
examples: In the presence of this type of compound, the addition of hydrogen bromide to alkenes is anti-Markovnikov

works.name: Diels-Alder Reaction
works.description: Alkenes serve as dienophiles in this key pericyclic reaction.

clues.clue: Alkene acts as the dienophile reacting with a conjugated diene to form cyclohexene
examples: simplest Diels-Alder reaction uses one of these compounds as the dienophile
combines a conjugated diene and a dienophile
the dienophile in the Diels-Alder reaction is often a compound with one of these functional groups

clues.clue: Woodward-Hoffmann rules explain stereochemistry of pericyclic reactions including Diels-Alder
examples: Kenichi Fukui developed the Frontier Molecular Orbital Theory to explain these rules

clues.clue: Bredt's rule: double bond cannot be placed at the bridgehead of bridged rings (angle/ring strain)
examples: This rule states that for bridged rings, a double bond cannot be placed at the bridgehead

works.name: Transition Metal Complexes with Alkenes
works.description: Alkenes as ligands in organometallic chemistry.

clues.clue: Dewar-Chatt-Duncanson model: describes how alkenes donate sigma electrons to metals and accept pi electrons (backbonding)
examples: Complexes of transition metals to these compounds are described by the Dewar-Chatt-Duncanson model
This model describes donation of electron density from ligands to transition metal d-orbitals

clues.clue: Zeise's salt: platinum bonded to three chlorines + one ethylene; first organometallic compound
examples: Platinum is covalently bonded to three chlorine atoms and complexed to one of these structures in Zeise's salt

clues.clue: Pi backbonding increases bond length of coordinated alkenes compared to free alkenes
examples: Pi backbonding leads to a small but measurable increase in this quantity for alkenes bound to metals

clues.clue: Alkene-metal complexes resemble cyclopropane rings
examples: these compounds both donate sigma electrons to a metal and accept pi electrons, which causes their complexes to resemble cyclopropane rings

clues.clue: Ziegler-Natta catalysts polymerize alkenes
examples: Ziegler-Natta catalysts polymerize these compounds
Formed from alkenes by using a Ziegler Natta catalyst

works.name: Cis-Trans / E-Z Isomerism
works.description: Configurational isomerism arising from restricted rotation of the double bond.

clues.clue: Can't rotate → configurational isomerism denoted E-Z or cis-trans
examples: Since these structures can't rotate, they may display configurational isomerism denoted E-Z or cis-trans
classified as E or Z

clues.clue: Unsaturated fatty acids have bent shape ('unsaturated')
examples: Fatty acids with these structures have a bent shape and are called 'unsaturated'

clues.clue: Cis-trans isomerization breaks stiff pi bond, allowing 180° rotation
examples: This reaction temporarily breaks a stiff pi bond, allowing its substituents to rotate 180 degrees

clues.clue: Retinal cis-trans isomerization triggers opsin in the retina (vision)
examples: When a conjugated aldehyde undergoes this reaction, an opsin is triggered in the retina

clues.clue: Ethylene: simplest alkene (C2H4); plant hormone that stimulates flower development and fruit ripening
examples: the simplest one of these compounds stimulates the development of flowers and ripening of fruit
simplest of which is ethylene

clues.clue: Hydroformylation: aldehydes synthesized industrially by carbonylating alkenes (cobalt + 4 CO ligands)
examples: Aldehydes are synthesized industrially by carbonylating these compounds
Cobalt coordinated to four carbonyl ligands is used to convert instances of this functional group into aldehydes

clues.clue: Wacker process: ethylene oxidized to acetaldehyde using PdCl2 catalyst, regenerated with CuCl2
examples: Ethene can also be oxidised to form acetaldehyde through this cyclic process, in the presence of a palladium (II) chloride catalyst

clues.clue: HFC refrigerants with alkene structures have reduced global warming potential
examples: Hydrofluorocarbon refrigerants with these structures added have greatly reduced global warming potential

clues.clue: Conjugated polyenes serve as chromophores; absorbance modeled as particle in a box
examples: commonly serve as chromophores whose absorbances can be modeled as a particle in a box

clues.clue: 2000 Nobel (Heeger, MacDiarmid, Shirakawa): conductive polymer containing alkenes (polyacetylene)
examples: 2000 Nobel in Chemistry was awarded to Heeger, MacDiarmid, and Shirakawa for their work in studying a conductive polymer containing this functional group

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How alkenes are typically identified at the end of tossups.

clues.clue: Functional group with a carbon-carbon double bond
examples: name this functional group in which two carbons are joined by a double bond
these unsaturated hydrocarbons with at least one carbon-carbon double bond

clues.clue: Also known as olefins
examples: also known as olefins

clues.clue: Contain sp2-hybridized carbon atoms
examples: contain at least two sp2-hybridized carbon atoms
two sp2-carbon atoms linked together by a double bond

clues.clue: General formula CnH2n
examples: expressed using the general formula CnH2n

clues.clue: Compounds with 1,5-pattern of two alkenes undergo [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement (Cope)
examples: Compounds containing two of this functional group in a 1,5-pattern can interconvert in a [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement

comprehensive_summary: Alkenes (olefins) are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond with sp2-hybridized carbons. The most commonly clued aspects are: (1) Named reactions — Grubbs/Schrock metathesis is the most frequent, followed by Zaitsev's rule for elimination selectivity, McMurry coupling, and Wittig/HWE/Peterson olefinations. (2) Addition reactions — Markovnikov's rule (HX/water), anti-Markovnikov hydroboration-oxidation, bromine decolorization test, Sharpless dihydroxylation with OsO4, oxymercuration, and epoxidation. (3) Organometallic chemistry — the Dewar-Chatt-Duncanson model describes alkene-metal bonding, Zeise's salt is the classic example, and Ziegler-Natta catalysts polymerize alkenes. (4) Diels-Alder — alkenes act as dienophiles. (5) E-Z isomerism from restricted rotation. (6) Industrial — ethylene as plant hormone, hydroformylation, Wacker process, conductive polymers (2000 Nobel). Power clues tend toward specific named reactions (Peterson, Julia, Still-Gennari), the Wacker process, and applications like the 2000 Nobel.
recursive_suggestions: Alkynes — very frequently clued alongside alkenes, many tossups
Diels-Alder reaction — appears as its own answerline
Olefin metathesis — Grubbs/Schrock, Nobel Prize topic
Markovnikov's rule — likely its own answerline
